Speaker 1: This next tool will be so powerful if you need to teach anybody how to negotiate. Even,
again, I'm going to teach you in this simple tool, but most people negotiate wrong. Most
people negotiate with the desire to be heard. I know this, I've negotiated so many deals,
probably more than most. Again, I'm in my fifties and I've been in business since I was
17. I've negotiated, negotiated, negotiated, and I watched so many people, lack of a
better word, already said, do it wrong. They mess it up because people prepare. They
want to show their value, they want to show what they have and that's really not what
makes a winning negotiation. Usually this doesn't win.
This does. Listening is what allows you to negotiate on another level. I want to give you
an example. I dress like this all the time, T-shirt and jeans. Most all places I go, even if
I'm negotiating a $20 million deal, which I did not that long ago, and I was dressed just
like this and everybody was in suits and even the little fancy pocket ties and stuff, or I
don't even know how the handkerchief, I'm going to give you a 30,000 foot overview,
then we'll get to the tool. Most people when they go to meetings, and I've watched
these have observed them, is everybody has their ... they're ready. They can't wait to
pounce and share. This is why we should do it. This is why we could close the deal. This
is why you should work with me. This is why you should get my business or give me your
money or give me your thing. Right? And they're all ready. A lot of times people don't
know what the other people are thinking.
So remember these two phrases, they've changed everything and you'll learn more of
this when we get to the marketing and selling of your mastermind. You're going to die.
That's where I think I'm doing good now, I hope you think I'm doing good now and
sharing these tools, wait until we get to marketing and then you'll see a whole nother
level of me, but what most people do is share what's on their mind. When really people
will buy from you, say yes to you, when they feel understood, not when they
understand you. Did you hear what I just said? That might've been worth hundreds of
millions of dollars to me. People will buy from you and say yes to you when they feel
understood, not when they understand you. Most people want to say, "Understand me,
understand me, understand me, understand me."
Yeah, but did you ever have to have somebody to do that? You're like, you don't even
know what I'm thinking, right? The other thing people will say, yes to you or buy from
you when you enter conversations already going on in their mind, we want to, not we,
so many people want to push down. You need this. You need to for us to work together,
you need this deal. People won't do what they need in life. People do what they want. A

sugar or go to the gym unless they want to. We do what we want in life. So how does

way.
Let me give you an example. I go to a meeting, I usually dress like this. I like being
understated. I like walking in and not saying much and I will listen because people are

time, they'll vomit up every desire they want and every fear they have. I'll just keep

He hasn't said a word. I'm listening and if it slows down I might say, "Hey guys, I'm a
little confused." And these are exact words. I'll say, "I'm a little confused. If this was a
year from now and this deal went great, we did business together, I gave you the
money, or I gave you the product or whatever it is. If we did this together and it was the
best year of our lives and the deal was perfect. What does that look like to you guys?"
That'll spark more.
Oh, it looks like this to me. It looks like this to ... Then I'll say little things like, "Well what
are you afraid of?" Or, "What's your biggest worry?" Or, "Why would you be excited to
have this deal happen?" If you just sit there quiet enough and I am almost to where it's
uncomfortable, guess what they'll do? They will expose all of their fears. They'll expose
all of their desires and they will give you every tool you need to make the deal happen.
Listen, there was a Harvard study where people would go to a therapist and they would
share in the first half hour, everything wrong with their life, everything they're worried
about it. The second half hour, the therapist would simply repeat back everything they
said. Yeah. You know, your wife doesn't give you the credibility that you desire. Oh my
God, you're right. When people left, they gave the therapist, 95% of the people gave the
therapist, credit for being smarter than them by simply repeating back what they told
them, right?
So when it comes to negotiating, you want to be, and you want to teach people in your
room, in your mastermind or virtual, that the key to winning is to hearing and listening
about their fears, listening about their desires, listening about their goals. Then you
literally can decide if that's their goals and that's their fears. You might say, "I didn't
even want this deal. There's no way." Or you say, "It's a year from now, it's the best of
year of life. What does that look like?" They tell you something, it's like, that's so far
from my goals. You win by walking away.
If you want the deal, you'll win because the first thing is, and if you can go to the sheet,
the first thing is what is their ideal outcome? What's their ideal outcome? What's their
desires? Where do they want this to go? If it was perfect for them, what would that look
like? You're going to want to teach people that you should be writing this down in your
journal as they're talking, as you're listening, oh, they want to hit 10 million in sales. Oh,
they want to merge with our company. Oh, they want this. Oh, they want that. So you
hear all that and you could make little stars next to, oh, that can make that work. That
one's a hell no and go through those.
Simultaneously, you should be writing down what their biggest fears are. What are their
biggest worries? If this deal doesn't happen, they could go bankrupt. If this deal doesn't
happen, Bob over here doesn't get the raise, Sally over here doesn't get to be partners
in the company. You start writing down their biggest desires and their biggest fears
because you want to live in their mind. You want to enter conversations they're already
having. You want them to feel understood. Does that make sense?
Then you want to craft, in your mind, if this was a win for both of us, it was a year from
now we're looking back and it's just going amazing. What does that look like? When you

take into consideration what their goals are, what their fears are, and then of course
you know what you want already. You know what's an unacceptable, I can't do the deal
without this. There's some leverage in the middle. There's some flexibility in the middle,
and then there's things that you can't have happen, right? These are a must, these are

as well. So now you take, theirs, fears and their desires, you figure out yours, and you
craft what would a perfect win-win look like?
The whole time I'm processing this, I don't really do this when I'm in a meeting, this is
just a tool to teach the people you're following, but I'm processing all this. I go, this
would be a win if I gave them 7% and or whatever the numbers are, right? So now that
you're listening and observing what their biggest fears are, what their ideal outcome is,
you already know what yours are. It might have been a little bit changed by listening,
but now it's time to just write them down quick. You already know what they are in your
head, but write down what is your ideal outcome? What are your biggest fears?
So really in your out ideal outcome, it's like what are the things that are a must, this has
to happen, I have to get 7% override, I have to get this deal, I have to ... What is the
must? Then in your fears, I would just put the things down that are unacceptable. I can't
do this deal if they're going to want me to fly to their office once a week, I can't do this
deal ... Now you kind of have a global picture. So the next piece that you go down is the
best question of all is, can I make this deal work? You might get enough information and
say, "I'm done. I can't do this. I can walk away from this." Right? Or you say, "If this was
a year from now and we look back and this was the best deal on the planet for both of
us, it was a win-win. What would that deal look like?"
You're taking into consideration their ideal outcome, your ideal outcome, your fears,
and now just craft just a bullet point. You're not writing a contract. This is just
negotiating and again, this should become second nature. You're not going to have to
bring the sheet when you negotiate. This is just the framework. You're teaching people a
framework. I'm teaching you a framework. So now what's the ideal outcome? It's a year
from now, best year, but it's like, okay, we'd do the 7% override. I wouldn't have to go
to their office. We'd double our sales, we impact more lives. Then that's your big
picture. Then you go down to the next section and go, okay, these are the deal points
I'm willing to do. You can put one through five deal points, like this is a must, that's a
must, this is unacceptable. You could do that in this little section. Lastly, what's your
final offer? What are you going to say to them?
Now, again, is this a sheet you're going to bring in and fill out when you're negotiating?
No. This is to give you a framework on how to negotiate in a different way. Instead of
negotiating from, this is who I am, this is what I have, this is what we should do. It's
more of, let me hear what your fears, let me hear your desires, let me let you feel
understood. Let me enter conversation you're already having, let me see what you want
and let me see if I can match that in a way where I still win or make it a win for both
parties.
